scenario: Sentry work session - audit every production subsystem that depends on the core issue model before reworking its contract

map_quality.rationale: The answer correctly identifies Sentry as a Django/Python + React/TypeScript monorepo, names the per-subsystem backend packages (issues, models, eventstream, integrations, notifications, workflow_engine, sentry_apps, services, tasks, api), pins the core model to src/sentry/models/group.py:704, and lists 8 additional concrete entry points spanning API, eventstore, workflow, and notifications — enough for a downstream agent to act with targeted lookups rather than fresh exploration.

specificity.rationale: Every entry in the orientation_map carries a concrete file:line and a short description (e.g. 'src/sentry/models/group.py:704 core issue model class Group', 'src/sentry/api/serializers/models/group.py:118 backend issue response contract'), tied to actual class names and package paths rather than vague labels.

justification.rationale: The answer labels Group the 'core issue model' and lists dependent subsystems in the orientation_map, but never explicitly states WHY this model is the restructuring target — that it is the deduplicated-issue aggregate every triage, notification, workflow, and API surface holds a reference to; the causal argument is implicit at best.

uncertainty.rationale: A trailing 'limitations' block notes truncated reads and scope exclusions ('The final map excludes tests and migrations'), but this is generic and not specific to the orientation step; no part of the orientation map is individually flagged as unverified or tentative.

map_quality.rationale: The answer maps status/substatus lifecycle at multiple line anchors (182, 220, 736, 1207), the project FK (711), message/culprit metadata (721-722), every major field with exact line numbers, and crucially the four reference patterns other code uses: full Group object, string issue ID, integer group_id in queues/eventstore, and FlexibleForeignKey — exactly what an agent needs to know before restructuring the contract.

specificity.rationale: Every entry is pinned to src/sentry/models/group.py with an exact line number and the actual Python field or method name (e.g., substatus at :736, qualified_short_id at :842, by_qualified_short_id at :371); nothing is described vaguely.

justification.rationale: The reference_contract_notes names the four holding patterns and lists the 'most fragile fields,' but does not explain per-field why fragility exists (e.g., does not connect status/substatus to alert-rule filters or notification copy that reads them); the coupling reasoning is deferred to later steps rather than surfaced here where the contract is being mapped.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer flags truncated reads in the limitations section but never flags specific uncertainty about the contract itself — for example it doesn't note which GroupManager methods or properties it could not confirm due to truncation, leaving the agent with false confidence in completeness.

steps.step: Enumerate every production dependent of the issue model

map_quality.rationale: The answer covers the obvious API/serializer layer, ingest, eventstream, satellite models, workflow_engine, notifications, integrations, and frontend types with an extensive flat list, but entirely omits the email-digest context (digests/utils.py), the AI night-shift TriageResult (tasks/seer/night_shift/models.py), the three sentry-app external-issue builders (external_issue_creator / issue_link_creator / issue_link_requester), and rules/history/base.py specifically — these are precisely the 'scattered production code far from the model' the rubric targets, and their absence means the agent would still need broad exploratory passes over those subsystems.

specificity.rationale: The step3_flat_dependents block is a concrete file:line list (e.g. 'src/sentry/services/eventstore/models.py:698', 'src/sentry/workflow_engine/models/workflow_fire_history.py:16') with no vague groupings, satisfying the one-dependent-per-line:file:line requirement for everything that was found.

justification.rationale: The flat list in step3_flat_dependents contains 200+ individually pinned entries rather than collapsed subsystem summaries, matching the rubric's 'one dependent per line' requirement; no items are hand-waved as 'the various serializers' or similar.

uncertainty.rationale: The only hedge is a generic 'limitations' note ('Read-only sandbox; filters out unrelated non-issue uses of the word group') that does not flag the specific subsystem gaps (digests, AI triage, sentry-app builders); the answer implies near-completeness, which would lead the agent to skip exploring those subsystems.

map_quality.rationale: The answer produces nine coherent subsystem buckets (satellite models, API/serialization, ingest/eventstore/eventstream, post-process/lifecycle, workflow engine, notifications, integrations/sentry-apps, rules/release/export, frontend) each with coupling descriptions, but the email-digest subsystem (digests/utils.py) is entirely absent, and the AI night-shift triage subsystem (tasks/seer/night_shift/models.py) never appears in the grouping — two of the cross-cutting non-obvious clusters the rubric names explicitly as completeness tests.

specificity.rationale: Almost every entry is pinned to a concrete file:line with the class or field named ('stores Group FK', 'task boundary accepts group_id and GroupState', 'Celery/taskworker boundary accepts group_id') — the coupling description is per-entry rather than per-subsystem, which gives the agent exactly what it needs.

justification.rationale: The grouping distinguishes durable FK embedding from queue-boundary passing from transient field reads (e.g. 'stores unique Group FK for archive/snooze state' vs 'reads event.group.times_seen_with_pending' vs 'serializes group_id/group_ids and merge/unmerge/delete operations'), which reflects real coupling depth; the 'rules_alerting_search_release_replay_export' bucket collapses six genuinely different coupling styles under one label, weakening the analysis for the agent there.

uncertainty.rationale: The 'limitations' section notes that tests and migrations are excluded and that unrelated 'group' uses were filtered, but the answer never flags a dependent it was unsure how to place, nor does it acknowledge that the digests and AI-triage subsystems may exist but were not found — a false-completeness signal the agent would act on incorrectly.

steps.step: Assess the blast radius of the contract change

map_quality.rationale: The answer delivers 12 ranked entries with product-flow and coupling characterization across ingest, API, eventstore/eventstream, workflow engine, notifications, integrations, merge/reprocessing, and frontend — a solid sweep. However, the AI night-shift TriageResult (tasks/seer/night_shift/models.py), the email-digest context (digests/utils.py), and the alert-rule fire history (rules/history/base.py) are absent from the ranked list despite being called out by the rubric as exactly the scattered dependents that matter; their omission leaves the agent under-informed about at least three subsystems before acting.

specificity.rationale: Every ranked entry is pinned to a concrete file:line with an actual coupling description ('queue boundary passes group_id and GroupState', 'exports the Group contract as JSON', 'serializes group_id/group_ids and merge/unmerge/delete operations') rather than vague subsystem labels — an agent can act on these directly.

justification.rationale: The top four entries have strong per-entry justifications ('breaking it silently misgroups or misstates all new events', 'loud UI/API breakage plus client compatibility issues') but the rationale grows thinner for ranks 7–10 (e.g., notificationmessage above merge/reprocessing with only 'can duplicate, fail to update, or lose open-period context' vs. 'historical references and derived rows point at wrong issue'), and the distinction between HIGH and MEDIUM-HIGH is asserted rather than argued.

uncertainty.rationale: The only uncertainty signal is a footer limitation ('Read-only sandbox; no code changes or verification tests were run') that applies globally; no individual ranked entry is flagged as uncertain, and the AI triage subsystem and digest context are absent without acknowledgment that they may be missing — the agent could wrongly treat the list as complete.

map_quality.rationale: The answer lists 12 plausible non-obvious dependents (reprocessing2, replay counts, data-export, groupStore, etc.) but entirely misses the three the rubric treats as canonical: the AI night-shift TriageResult (tasks/seer/night_shift/models.py), the email-digest context (digests/utils.py), and workflow_engine/types.py — which the rubric singles out because it defines its own unrelated nested `Group` enums that make a text search actively misleading. The TYPE_CHECKING import pattern (rules/history/base.py, workflow_engine/types.py, notifications/base.py) that makes a runtime-import sweep blind is never surfaced as a pattern. The specific sentry-app external-issue builders (external_issue_creator / issue_link_creator / issue_link_requester) are collapsed into platformexternalissue.py, missing the builder files.

specificity.rationale: Items that are present carry concrete file:line anchors and brief labels, so the format is correct; the gap is that the most discriminating entries (AI triage, digest context, TYPE_CHECKING imports, workflow_engine name collision) are absent, leaving the agent with an incomplete but formatted list.

justification.rationale: Explanations given are mostly 'lives in a different subsystem' (e.g., 'Lives under notifications, not issues') rather than the mechanistic reasons the rubric targets: 'if TYPE_CHECKING means a runtime import sweep never sees it' and 'this file defines its own Group enums so a text search returns 50 false positives.' Without these mechanistic reasons, the agent still cannot know which search strategies to distrust.

uncertainty.rationale: The trailing 'limitations' block notes the map excludes tests and migrations but does not flag that TYPE_CHECKING-only imports or name-collision files could cause systematic misses — precisely the uncertainty the rubric asks the answer to declare.

map_quality.rationale: The map is broadly comprehensive and file:line-anchored with a grouped subsystem structure and a 50-entry verification_targets list, but it drops several dependents the rubric specifically requires: digests/utils.py (email-digest context) is absent entirely, tasks/seer/night_shift/models.py (AI TriageResult) is absent, the three sentry-app external-issue builder classes (external_issue_creator/issue_link_creator/issue_link_requester) are absent, and workflow_engine/types.py (WorkflowEventData, WorkflowGroupHistory, plus the embedded Group enum name collision) is absent; the verification checklist is 7 generic rules rather than per-dependent action items, so a teammate still needs to triage those gaps before landing the PR.

specificity.rationale: File:line citations are present throughout and coupling is briefly stated per entry (e.g., 'src/sentry/eventstream/base.py:29 GroupState TypedDict compatibility'), but class/structure names are frequently omitted — entries like 'src/sentry/notifications/models/notificationmessage.py:47 notification message row' or 'src/sentry/workflow_engine/models/detector_group.py:19 stores detector-to-issue one-to-one' lack the actual class name and field, leaving the agent to open the file to identify the coupling point.

justification.rationale: The 7-item checklist captures the main verification categories (queue skew, serializer/type pairing, status semantics, merge rewrites, Snuba identity), but it applies to the whole map rather than being threaded per dependent; individual entries in verification_targets largely just restate the coupling (e.g., 'ingest existing-group path', 'notification message row') without saying what specific assertion or migration test would prove the dependent is safe after the contract change.

uncertainty.rationale: The limitations block acknowledges a read-only sandbox and test/migration exclusions, but it does not flag the concrete gaps — no mention that digests, AI-triage, sentry-app builder classes, or workflow_engine/types.py were not found — implying false completeness rather than marking open items for the implementer to confirm.
